_all_text: Aquamarine aquamarine Be3Al2Si6O18 (with Fe2+/Fe3+) Blue color from Fe2+ ions in the structural channel sites. Unheated aquamarine often has a greenish tint from Fe3+ mixing with Fe2+ — heat treatment converts Fe3+ to Fe2+, producing a purer blue. Deep pure-blue specimens (Santa Maria) are most valued. The deepest-blue 'Santa Maria' color (from Santa Maria de Itabira, Brazil) is a benchmark color grade. Aquamarine is the sea captured in hexagonal beryl — pale to deep blue with the clarity of tropical water. The Dom Pedro aquamarine (Smithsonian, carved by Bernd Munsteiner) is the largest faceted aquamarine in the world (10,363 ct). Long prismatic crystal form with striated faces is characteristic of large rough specimens. Ancient mariners believed aquamarine was the sacred gemstone of mermaids, cast up from the sea floor. Carrying aquamarine on a sea voyage was thought to bring calm seas, prevent seasickness, and protect against drowning. In Roman tradition it was sacred to Neptune. The association with water, clarity, and truth persists in contemporary use. The 'Dom Pedro Aquamarine' (Smithsonian's National Gem Collection) — a 10,363 ct carved obelisk — is the largest faceted aquamarine in the world. the sea safe voyages clarity and calm communication and truth March and early spring Sailor's talisman for protection at sea — Roman and Greek traditions Glasses / magnifying lenses (beryl lenses used in medieval Europe) Jewelry since antiquity Carving and decorative arts (large crystals) aquamarine gemstone Santa Maria aquamarine blue beryl March birthstone sailor gemstone Dom Pedro aquamarine Be3Al2Si6O18 iron silicate variety beryl gemstone blue birthstone march hexagonal beryllium sea high-priority sea-glass-clarity tropical-water-blue sailor-talisman iron-sky-light clear-ocean-prism pale-blue blue-green blue
